Goal: Generating a 1-3% yield while leaving room for the underlying asset to grow.
Extracting the highest possible extrinsic premium when a stock is expected to trade sideways.
Collecting massive premium with deep downside protection when slightly bearish, sacrificing all upside.
Getting paid to wait for a target entry price below the current market value.
Maximizing premium collection while aggressively attempting to acquire the underlying asset.
Replicating a covered call using deep ITM LEAPS instead of equity to free up 70%+ of capital for other trades.
Looping CSPs and Covered Calls to continuously lower cost basis and generate yield regardless of market direction.
Buying shares and selling calls in one simultaneous block order to immediately lock in a lower entry price.
Hedging a long portfolio against catastrophic, systemic market crashes.
Selling OTM calls to entirely fund the purchase of OTM puts, creating a defined floor and ceiling for the asset.
Buying 1 call and selling 2 higher-strike calls to lower the breakeven point on a losing stock without risking more capital.
Margin Requirement: Margin for short calls / Own 100 shares

Goal: Selling a high-strike put to finance buying multiple lower-strike puts. Pays off massively in a crash, but loses in a mild dip.
Betting that realized volatility will outpace the market's implied volatility, regardless of price direction.
Trading the underlying stock against a long option position to lock in small profits as the Delta constantly shifts.
Margin Requirement: Cost of options + dynamic stock margin

Goal: Selling OTM calls and puts before an earnings report to profit from the rapid drop in Implied Volatility (IV crush) after the news.
Selling OTM calls and puts unhedged (naked) to maximize IV crush profits. Extremely high risk, requires heavy margin.
Using options to lock in the spread between an acquisition target's current price and the buyout price while hedging deal-failure risk.
Selling options on an Index (low vol) while buying options on its components (high vol), betting on the correlation breaking down.
Selling near-term options and buying longer-term options at the same strike to exploit the faster time decay (Theta) of the short-term contract.
Buying cheap, long-dated calls to gain massive asymmetric leverage on high-conviction macroeconomic trends.
Buying ITM options and selling OTM options to define risk while taking a heavily directional stance on a mispriced asset.
Buying an ATM call and selling an ATM put to exactly replicate holding 100 shares, often used to free up capital or play corporate actions.
